_comment: Curated embed seed for the Explore feed (Observe stage). Each entry becomes one full-width 'embed' card rendered as a lazy iframe. Sourced from open archives (Internet Archive / Musopen, public-domain or freely-streamable) and local Keystone games. Every card carries [why, source] per the Σ₀ Verify rule. To add/replace content, edit this file — no code change. Per-card key = 'source:embed:<slug>' so each panel earns or loses its slot independently (engagement or discarded). Verified live 2026-06-27.

embeds.title: What's New — Unisona 1.8
embeds.lore: The personal reasoning cockpit goes public — one name, one loop, a user-facing surface at every stage of Observe → Remember → Reason → Act → Verify → Converge.
embeds.why: The 1.8 milestone release, written to be read — links into every new cockpit

embeds.title: Kingdome of Hearts — Three Doors
embeds.lore: Seven stages, three doors each scene, no wrong choices. A dream journey through the Kingdome — each loop different.
embeds.why: Keystone's own choose-your-path game, served locally — the Three Doors loop made playable

embeds.title: T-Rex Runner
embeds.lore: A dinosaur outruns the void of a dead connection. The original offline myth — born the moment the network dies.
embeds.why: The Chrome offline classic, served locally — no network required

embeds.title: Prince of Persia (1990)
embeds.lore: Jordan Mechner rotoscoped his brother's run frame-by-frame into a desert palace. You have sixty minutes to cross it and reach her.
embeds.why: Emulated in-browser by the Internet Archive's MS-DOS library

embeds.title: The Oregon Trail (1990)
embeds.lore: Ford the river or caulk the wagon. A whole generation learned mortality to a green CRT glow — 'you have died of dysentery.'
embeds.why: Emulated in-browser by the Internet Archive's MS-DOS library

embeds.title: Frank Sinatra — Radio Years (1943–1964)
embeds.lore: 218 radio broadcasts of The Voice, transmitted across two decades and now in the public domain. Put it on; let the room change.
embeds.why: Public Domain Mark 1.0 — 218 streamable broadcasts

embeds.title: Mozart — Symphony No. 40 in G minor, K.550
embeds.lore: The G-minor symphony — restless, modern before its time. Musopen recorded it copyright-free so it could play anywhere, forever.
embeds.why: Musopen copyright-free recording (Columbia University Orchestra)

embeds.title: Pac-Man (1983)
embeds.lore: Namco, 1983. A yellow circle, four ghosts, and a maze that taught the whole world the words 'high score.'
embeds.why: Emulated in-browser by the Internet Archive's MS-DOS library

embeds.lore: Gabriele Cirulli built it in a single weekend and dropped it on GitHub under the MIT license. The internet lost the next month sliding tiles; it's been forked ten thousand times since.
embeds.why: MIT-licensed open-source game, served locally — no cross-origin frame

embeds.title: Night of the Living Dead (1968)
embeds.lore: Romero shot it for $114,000 in rural Pennsylvania. A missing copyright notice dropped the print into the public domain, where it quietly became everyone's nightmare.
embeds.why: Public-domain film, streamable in-page from the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Jazz Classics — Armstrong · Ellington · Fitzgerald
embeds.lore: Tight Like This, The Mooche, Basin Street Blues — the early recordings that aged into the public domain and never once stopped swinging.
embeds.why: Public-domain jazz, streamable in-page from the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Sonic the Hedgehog (1991)
embeds.lore: Sega's answer to Mario: a blue hedgehog with attitude and somewhere to be. Built to show off what the Genesis could do at full speed in 1991 — press Enter, then arrows + A/S/D.
embeds.why: Sega Genesis emulator, playable in-browser at the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Mario Bros. (1983, NES port)
embeds.lore: The 1983 game that first put 'Mario' in a title and handed him his brother Luigi. The original arcade cabinet isn't on the open internet, so this is the faithful NES port — same pipes, same POW block.
embeds.why: Playable NES emulator at the Internet Archive (arcade original is not openly available)
embeds.evidence_source: archive.org · mariobros_nes (emulator: nes; arcade original unavailable)

embeds.title: Super Mario Land (1989)
embeds.lore: Mario's first handheld outing — a small, strange kingdom called Sarasaland that shipped alongside the original Game Boy and sold eighteen million copies on the strength of curiosity alone.
embeds.why: Game Boy emulator, playable in-browser at the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Mike Tyson's Punch-Out!! (1987)
embeds.lore: Little Mac, five-foot-seven of pure pattern-recognition, punching up through a roster of giants. Nintendo licensed the heavyweight champion himself for the final bout — when the contract lapsed it quietly became plain 'Punch-Out!!' and Iron Mike became legend.
embeds.why: NES emulator, playable in-browser at the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Super Mario World (1991)
embeds.lore: The Super Nintendo's launch showcase. A green dinosaur named Yoshi, a cape that let Mario fly, and a Dinosaur Land map hiding 96 exits — the game that taught a whole generation what 'secret level' meant.
embeds.why: SNES emulator, playable in-browser at the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Super Mario 64 (1996)
embeds.lore: The leap into three dimensions. A castle, a triple-jump, and a camera you suddenly had to think about — in 1996 Nintendo reinvented what a platformer even was, on a cartridge. (Give the N64 emulator a moment to boot.)
embeds.why: Nintendo 64 emulator, playable in-browser at the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Final Fantasy Adventure (1991)
embeds.lore: Square's Game Boy action-RPG — released as Final Fantasy Gaiden in Japan, it grew into the whole Mana series. Real-time sword-and-magic adventure in four shades of grey. (The numbered Final Fantasy titles are lending-restricted on the Archive; this is the openly-playable Square FF.)
embeds.why: Game Boy emulator, playable in-browser at the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Nosferatu (1922)
embeds.lore: Murnau filmed Dracula without the rights, renamed the Count 'Orlok', and Stoker's widow sued the studio to ashes — a court ordered every print destroyed. The handful that survived made the first vampire of cinema immortal.
embeds.why: Public-domain silent film, streamable in-page from the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Steamboat Willie (1928)
embeds.lore: Mickey Mouse's debut, and the first cartoon with synchronized sound — Walt Disney whistling at the wheel of a steamboat. Disney's copyright held it for 95 years; on January 1, 2024 this version finally entered the public domain.
embeds.why: Entered the US public domain on Jan 1, 2024 — streamable in-page from the Internet Archive

embeds.title: Keystone Radio
embeds.lore: A retro Pip-Boy tuner spinning public-domain 1940s radio — Ink Spots, Bing Crosby, Ella, the songs that play at the end of the world. Press play; tune the dial.
embeds.why: Keystone's own retro player — six public-domain Fallout-era recordings, served locally
